{"step_by_step_thinking": "Profound hypotension can reduce coronary perfusion pressure (CPP), which is critical for delivering oxygen to the myocardium. Document [7] explicitly states that diastolic hypotension can impair coronary blood flow, placing patients at elevated risk for cardiovascular events, including myocardial infarction. Document [17] emphasizes that mean arterial pressure (MAP) should be maintained at or above 65–70 mmHg to ensure adequate coronary perfusion, and hypotension can lead to reduced CPP and myocardial oxygen delivery. Document [18] notes that in patients with stable coronary artery disease (CAD), SBP <120 mmHg and DBP <70 mmHg are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes, including death, suggesting a direct link between hypotension and adverse cardiac events. Additionally, Document [20] warns that hypotension in patients with coronary artery disease may exacerbate angina or myocardial infarction due to reduced perfusion. These findings collectively indicate that profound hypotensive events can impair coronary blood flow and lead to myocardial ischemia. Therefore, the evidence supports a causal relationship between profound hypotension and myocardial ischemic events in patients with coronary disease.", "answer_choice": "A"}
